html:
Toptec Corp. (KRX:108230)

Toptec is a messy, underfollowed Korean automation equipment stock that has been punished as if the business is permanently impaired. At the May 15 close of KRW 4,045, the equity value is about KRW 154 billion, roughly US$100 million. That is the wrong price for a company with KRW 387.8 billion of book equity, KRW 165.0 billion of cash, KRW 67.9 billion of debt, and roughly KRW 97 billion of net cash according to 2025 financial summaries (financials, EV bridge).

The market is right that 2025 was ugly. Revenue collapsed from KRW 473.6 billion in 2024 to KRW 171.0 billion in 2025, operating income swung to a KRW 3.1 billion loss, and net income was negative KRW 20.6 billion. A North American battery module contract was recently reduced or canceled, and the Samsung Display litigation remains a real overhang. ZDNet reported that the Supreme Court rejected Toptec’s appeal over Samsung Display’s OLED lamination patent validity, while a separate civil judgment ordered Toptec to pay KRW 11.6 billion plus interest, with appeals ongoing (ZDNet, follow-up).

That is why the stock trades at roughly 0.4x book and only about KRW 60 to 70 billion of enterprise value after net cash. The opportunity is that this is not a no-asset story. Toptec still has a real automation platform across battery cell, module, pack, logistics, display, semiconductor automation, and nanofiber or EMI materials through Lemon. The prior HBM TC-bonder thesis should be treated as unproven. Public HBM TC-bonder wins are showing up at Hanmi and Hanwha Semitech, not Toptec. The cleaner variant perception is battery automation and factory automation cyclicality, not a hidden HBM bonder win.

The forward setup is a project-cycle rebound. Toptec earned KRW 69.0 billion of operating income on KRW 601.7 billion of revenue in 2023. Even in 2024, before the full battery capex freeze hit, it produced KRW 12.5 billion of operating income and KRW 32.6 billion of net income. Today’s enterprise value is close to one year of 2023 operating profit. The stock does not need a heroic semiconductor re-rating. It needs battery and automation orders to normalize from the 2025 trough.

The market is also flattening the disclosure picture. The contract news is not all negative. Butler’s disclosure feed shows the April 2026 North American battery module-line termination, but it also shows the HSAGP Energy LLC secondary battery assembly-line contract of about KRW 76.3 billion extended to August 2026 and other module-line disclosures in the same cycle (Butler). The right interpretation is not “order book is dead.” It is “customer schedules are being renegotiated in a delayed North American battery capex cycle.” For an equipment supplier priced near liquidation value, delayed and re-scoped is very different from structurally obsolete.

The downside protection is not theoretical. Net cash is roughly KRW 2,550 per share, over 60% of the current stock price. Book value is roughly KRW 10,200 per share. The company still paid a KRW 100 dividend for 2025, a roughly 2.5% yield at the current price, despite the loss (dividend disclosure). That does not make the stock safe, but it means the market is assigning very little value to the actual operating franchise.

The upside torque comes from operating leverage. A base-case recovery to KRW 320 to 360 billion of 2026 to 2027 revenue at a 6% to 7% EBIT margin would produce KRW 19 to 25 billion of EBIT. Put only 8x to 9x on that, add discounted net cash, and the equity value is roughly KRW 290 to 320 billion, or about KRW 7,600 to 8,400 per share. A return toward the 2023 revenue and margin profile would make the current enterprise value look absurd.

The bear case is litigation, more contract cancellations, and continued EV battery capex deferrals. In that case, Toptec burns cash, the Samsung overhang grows, and the stock trades closer to cash value with a deep discount for governance and customer trust. The bull case is not just a cyclical rebound. It is a market reclassification from broken display supplier to net-cash battery automation platform with recoverable earnings power.

Targets from the May 15 price of KRW 4,045:

Bear: KRW 2,800, down 31%. More cancellations, no battery capex recovery, further Samsung legal drag, and cash is discounted heavily.

Base: KRW 8,000, up 98%. Revenue normalizes to KRW 320 to 360 billion, EBIT recovers to KRW 20 billion plus, and the market values the operating business at a still-modest multiple plus net cash.

Bull: KRW 16,000, up 296%. Battery automation orders reaccelerate, Toptec approaches its 2023 earnings power, materials stop being ignored, and the stock re-rates from distressed microcap to credible automation equipment supplier.
